briefing.opening: Good morning/afternoon. We will now begin the Climate Change Daily Briefing for May 21, 2026.

sections.title: 🌍 Strengthening the International Community's Legal Obligations for Climate Action
sections.content: The UN General Assembly, despite opposition from the United States, resolved to strengthen climate change measures and adopted a resolution by an overwhelming majority stating that inaction on climate change constitutes a violation of international law [1, 5, 11, 130, 135, 173]. This is regarded as a historic step that supports the International Court of Justice's (ICJ) advisory opinion on climate and reaffirms the legal obligation of each nation to take climate action [6, 131, 134, 159, 169, 171]. Some countries, including Australia, supported this groundbreaking UN climate change ruling, emphasizing the importance of international cooperation [151, 164]. Consequently, responding to climate change has now been established as a legal obligation, moving beyond a mere policy choice.

sections.title: ⚠️ Real Threats of Climate Change: Ecological and Societal Impacts
sections.content: The impacts of climate change are emerging as real threats both domestically and internationally. Domestically, the Gwangneungyo-gangkkot (Cypripedium japonicum) in Deogyusan bloomed two weeks earlier than usual, indicating a disruption to the 'flower clock' [2]. Internationally, the probability of deadly pre-monsoon heatwaves in South Asia has tripled [137], and the UK House of Lords warned that the country could face a daily water deficit of 5 billion liters by 2055 [172]. Furthermore, reports continue to emerge about cultural heritage sites in the Arctic region being rapidly damaged due to climate change [141, 145, 150], and research has warned that climate change could trigger a massive surge in cholera cases [153]. These phenomena reaffirm the widespread and severe impacts of climate change on ecosystems, public health, and society as a whole.

sections.title: 🇰🇷 Multifaceted Efforts for Carbon Neutrality in Korea
sections.content: In Korea, various efforts are actively underway to achieve carbon neutrality and respond to the climate crisis. The Korean Red Cross and the Net-Zero Climate Foundation have signed an agreement for joint action [7], and Jeongeup City aims to realize a sustainable carbon-neutral and climate-resilient city by 2026 [8, 18]. The government and local authorities are strengthening climate disaster response systems in preparation for heatwaves and heavy rainfall [13, 49, 56, 61], and are promoting active community-level responses, such as expanding resident-participatory climate crisis projects [16, 42]. The Ministry of Climate is accelerating technology and industry development, including initiating technology development to utilize soil as a carbon sink [21, 53], and recruiting excellent environmental companies in 2026 to identify green businesses that will lead carbon neutrality [20, 35, 45, 46, 50, 66]. Furthermore, Incheon City's 'Forest of Hope' creation project in Mongolia [14, 32] and Jeonbuk Self-Governing Province's achievement of exceeding its greenhouse gas reduction targets [65] demonstrate positive outcomes of domestic carbon neutrality efforts.

sections.title: 🌱 Expansion of Renewable Energy and Current Challenges
sections.content: The transition to renewable energy is receiving strong support both domestically and internationally. More than 8 out of 10 citizens support the expansion of renewable energy, recognizing it as key to power stability and energy security [72, 81, 88, 89, 92, 98, 100, 112, 128]. The government is laying the policy groundwork by setting a 100 GW target for 2030 and establishing a basic renewable energy plan, including restructuring the RPS market [76, 129]. Industrial investment is also active, with companies like Hanwha Q CELLS supplying domestically produced modules for Korea's largest 400 MW solar power project [95, 99, 108, 110, 114, 116, 117, 120, 122, 124, 125, 127]. However, the expansion of renewable energy comes with several challenges. Renewable energy self-sufficiency rates are significantly low in metropolitan area local governments with high power consumption [70, 73, 74, 80, 82, 106, 119], and there is a severe imbalance, with self-sufficiency rates varying by up to 729 times between regions [78]. Furthermore, concerns about power loss due to insufficient transmission grids [83, 94], waste disposal issues such as discarded panels [77, 109], and the potential impact on small and medium-sized solar power companies [126] are critical problems that must be addressed during the renewable energy transition.

briefing.closing: Today's briefing has highlighted the strengthening of the international community's legal obligations in responding to climate change, multifaceted domestic efforts, and both the promise and challenges of the renewable energy transition. We urge your continued attention and action to overcome the climate crisis, and with that, we conclude today's climate briefing.
